data.title: STOXX inches up, heads for small weekly gain
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 up 0.1%
  • Autos lead, Media sector lags
  • BoJ keeps rates unchanged, begins unwind of ETFs
  • Wall St futures inch lower

STOXX INCHES UP, HEADS FOR SMALL WEEKLY GAIN

Europe's STOXX 600 is a touch higher on Friday and heading for a small weekly rise, after a busy week that has seen a Fed rate cut and the Bank of Japan open the door to a rate hike later this year.

The pan-European benchmark is up 0.2% at around 555 points. For the week, it's up 0.1%.

Breadth is positive, with advancers outnumbering decliners by about 2:1.

Germany's DAX is up 0.3%, France's CAC 40 is up 0.7% and Britain's FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 is up 0.1%.

Autos (.SXAP) lead the way, helped by Stellantis after shares were upgraded at Berenberg.

Media (.SXMP) and tech (.SX8P) sectors are laggards, but the latter is still heading for a gain of over 5% this week, its biggest weekly jump since September.

Here's your opening snapshot:

(Samuel Indyk)

*****

EARLIER ON LIVE MARKETS

EUROPE BEFORE THE BELL: FUTURES HOLD AS HECTIC WEEK DRAWS TO A CLOSE CLICK HERE

BOJ HOLDS, WITH A HAWKISH TWIST CLICK HERE

data.title: UK polymer maker Victrex slips after Jefferies downgrades to 'hold'
data.description:
** Shares of UK-based high-performance polymer manufacturer Victrex fall nearly 1.4% to 724p

** Jefferies downgrades stock rating to "hold" from "buy"

** Cuts PT to 650p from 830p, new PT represents an 11.44% downside to the stock's last close

** Brokerage says Victrex is unlikely to grow its profit before tax (PBT) in FY26 because medical product volumes are still weak

** The strong growth in VAR (value-added resellers) is actually hurting margins due to a less favorable product mix, adds Jefferies

** Stock rated "hold" on average; median PT is 880p, per data compiled by LSEG

** As of last close, VCTX is down ~35% this year

data.title: Bata India Announces Trading Window Closure from October 1, 2025
data.description:
Bata India Limited has announced the closure of its trading window for dealing in the securities of the company, effective from October 1, 2025.

In compliance with the SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015, the trading window will remain closed for 48 hours following the declaration of the unaudited financial results for the quarter and half year ending September 30, 2025. This closure applies to all designated persons as defined by the company's code of conduct.

The company has requested that all concerned parties take note of this closure.

The terms used in this announcement carry the same meaning as ascribed to them in the said code.

Bata India Limited is a listed entity on both the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) and the National Stock Exchange (NSE). The BSE security code for Bata India is 500043, and the NSE symbol is BATAINDIA. The Calcutta Stock Exchange Limited (CSE) scrip code is 10000003.

Bata India Limited is registered with the Corporate Identity Number (CIN) L19201WB1931PLC007261. The company’s registered office is located at 27B, Camac Street, 1st Floor, Kolkata-700016, West Bengal.

The company’s contact information includes telephone number (033) 22895796 and fax number (033) 22895748. Inquiries can be directed to the email address in-customer.service@bata.com, and further information is available on the website www.bata.in.

NITIN BAGARIA, AVP - Company Secretary & Compliance Officer, has digitally signed the announcement.

 Bata India Limited is registered with the Corporate Identity Number (CIN) L19201WB1931PLC007261. The company’s registered office is located at 27B, Camac Street, 1st Floor, Kolkata-700016, West Bengal.

data.title: U.K. Public Borrowing Data Suggests Need to Raise Taxes at Autumn Budget — Market Talk
data.description: U.K. public-sector borrowing data for August came in worse than expected, suggesting the need for the government to raise taxes at the autumn budget on Nov. 26, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Elliott Jordan-Doak says in a note. Public borrowing increased to 18 billion pounds in August from 14.4 billion pounds in the same period a year ago, and above the 13.5 billion pounds consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ poll. U.K. Treasury Chief Rachel Reeves is expected to fill the fiscal deficit with a combination of "stealth and sin tax increases, along with some smaller spending cuts," Jordan-Doak says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Asian Economies, Assets Set to Gain From Fed's Easing — Market Talk
data.description: Asian economies and assets are likely to benefit from the Fed's decision to ease earlier this week, say economists and strategists at Manulife Investment Management. The Fed's move is expected to bring positive momentum for emerging markets, while the resumption of its easing cycle could loosen financial conditions, boost investor sentiment, which bodes well for risk assets. Asia can attract continued capital inflows as the Fed's cut supports risk appetite. "With lower inflationary pressures and a weaker U.S. dollar, EM Asia currencies have the scope to catch up in an environment where volatility remains low, especially for high yielders in the region," they say.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Greek current account surplus widens in July
data.description:
Greece recorded a current account surplus of 938 million euros ($1.1 billion) in July, up from a surplus of 210 million euros in the same month a year earlier, data from the Bank of Greece showed on Friday.

($1 = 0.8500 euros)

data.title: German Bund Yields Hit Two-Week High as Debt Issuance Rises
data.description:
German 10-year Bund yields climbed toward 2.75%, their highest level since September 2, after a volatile week shaped by larger debt issuance plans and major central bank decisions.

On Thursday, the German finance agency announced that Q4 issuance will increase by €15 billion compared with its December forecast, citing higher spending needs for infrastructure and defense.

Agency head Tammo Diemer noted strong investor demand for Bunds, partly supported by France’s ongoing budget crisis.

Meanwhile, protests continued across France in opposition to government spending cuts, while Italy is expected to receive a ratings upgrade from Fitch later on Friday.

In monetary policy, the BoJ kept interest rates unchanged but signaled a gradual unwinding of its ETF and REIT holdings, suggesting a possible shift toward reducing monetary stimulus sooner than expected.

Earlier this week, the Fed cut borrowing costs for the first time since December, while the BoE left rates unchanged.

data.title: Belarus expels Czech diplomat in tit-for-tat move
data.description:
Belarus' foreign ministry said on Friday that it had ordered a Czech diplomat to leave the country and summoned a senior Polish diplomat for a meeting, after both countries expelled Belarusian diplomatic staff.

The Czech Republic and Poland this month expelled Belarusian diplomats for espionage. Poland also arrested another person it alleged was a Belarusian spy.

Belarus, a close ally of Russia in its war against Ukraine, has been under Western sanctions since Minsk cracked down on protests against the 2020 election, in which official results gave longtime leader Alexander Lukashenko a victory not recognised by the opposition or most Western countries.

In a statement, the ministry said the expulsion was "the result of years of cultivated bias against Belarus by the Czech authorities."

Belarus this month hosted major military exercises with the participation of Russian forces.

data.title: USD/JPY: Yen Gains Against Dollar as Bank of Japan Keeps Rates Steady, Plans Big ETF Sales
data.description:
Key points:
  • Yen climbs against greenback
  • Markets digest BoJ ETF plans
  • Rate hold comes after Fed’s cut

No surprises on rate decision. But the bank’s move to start unloading its balance sheet shocked Japanese stocks and sent the yen higher… briefly.

💪 Yen Strenghtens on BoJ Plans

  • The pair shifted lower early Friday after the Bank of Japan said it’s keeping interest rates unchanged at 0.5%. The move was widely expected as Japan continues to struggle to get its economy off the ground.
  • While there wasn’t any talk of rate hikes (something many are secretly hoping for), Japan’s central bank said it’s planning to start unloading big chunks of the exchange-traded funds it's holding on its balance sheet.
  • How much exactly? About ¥335 billion ($2.4 billion) of ETFs and real estate investment trusts (REITs) a year. The BoJ has been purchasing ETFs and REITs for about 15 years as a way to ease its monetary policy and beat deflation. The book value of these holdings on the balance sheet is currently at ¥37.1 trillion ($250 billion).

👀 Reactions Across the Board

  • The Japanese yen initially shot higher, sending the dollar-yen lower by 0.3% to ¥147.20. The pump was short-lived and before the European session kicked off the pair was back near breakeven for the day.
  • Japanese stocks immediately dropped when the news got out with the Nikkei 225 sliding more than 1.6% for the day.
  • Apparently, the shock reaction was more or less contained. The Bank of Japan has long been expected to do something about its ultra-loose monetary policy. If policymakers are reluctant to hike rates, then unloading the balance sheet might be the next best thing? Not exactly.

🎁 Time for a Fresh Perspective?

  • The plans to offload the balance sheet weren’t supposed to happen right now. Some see this as a sign that members of the central bank committee are getting impatient and looking for ways to start unwinding the easy policies that have made the country go for decades.
  • In fact, two members voted to hike rates while seven members opted for a hold. The internal disagreement sparked speculation that the bank could be tilting toward a hike by the end of the year.
  • Analysts are quick to point out that the pressure to move is already there – at least two members will likely vote for the hike at the next meeting.
  • The BoJ’s rate decision came after the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates on Wednesday and after the Bank of England kept rates steady Thursday with two dissenting voices wanting a cut.

data.title: U.K. Miners Gain on Higher Gold Prices — Market Talk
data.description: London-listed miners gain in early morning European trading as gold ticks higher. Gold futures are up 0.2% at $3,685.20 a troy ounce and nearly 10% higher this month on U.S. interest rate cut hopes and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop. Endeavour Mining and Fresnillo are leading the FTSE 100 index risers, up 3% and 2.5%, respectively. Anglo American shares are up 1.3%, while Antofagasta shares are trading 1% higher (aimee.look@wsj.com)

data.title: Oil Poised for Modest Weekly Gain as Russian Supply Risks Remain in Focus — Market Talk
data.description:
0800 GMT - Oil prices are on track for a modest weekly gain as a firmer U.S. dollar weighs on sentiment and markets await more clarity on Western sanctions against Russia. In early trade, Brent crude is down 0.2% to $67.28 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.4% to $62.99 a barrel. Both benchmarks are up only 0.5% this week. "Despite modest weekly gains, crude remains stuck in a narrow range since early August, weighed down by bearish fundamentals including OPEC+'s accelerated output return," says Soojin Kim from MUFG. However, concerns persist over potential disruptions to Russian supplies following recent Ukrainian attacks on key energy infrastructure and President Trump's call for NATO allies to halt purchases of Russian oil. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - Bitcoin falls slightly as traders take profits after the cryptocurrency reached a one-month high in the previous session. The Federal Reserve resumed cutting interest rates on Wednesday. Bitcoin initially fell after the decision as Fed Chair Jerome Powell pointed to more measured path of rate cuts than anticipated. However, bitcoin later reversed higher as the Fed still pointed to further rate cuts that are positive for risky assets. "It's worth noting that we are now well past the midpoint of a September that has historically been one of the weakest months for crypto performance," LMAX Group strategist Joel Kruger says in a note. Bitcoin falls 0.5% to $117,012 after hitting a high of $117,948 Thursday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - U.K. public-borrowing data is going to lead to calls about the sustainability of public finances, and the need for tax rises at the upcoming government budget, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Net borrowing at 18.0 billion pounds notched the highest August borrowing figure in five years, suggesting the U.K. is borrowing at the same rates as during the Covid era, she says. For the fiscal year so far--April to August--it marked the second-highest borrowing level since records began in the 1990s. The data could also lead to questions being asked about the size of the U.K. state, as well as the cost of public services versus private ones, she says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0741 GMT - Gold prices rise in early trade after settling lower in the previous session, weighed down by a stronger U.S. dollar and cautious optimism surrounding the Federal Reserve's policy outlook. "The Fed's tone on future rate cuts was less dovish than expected after its September meeting concluded," ANZ analysts say. Gold futures edge 0.2% higher to $3,685.20 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar index trades 0.1% higher at 97.45. Still, the precious metal remains up nearly 10% this month and is expected to continue benefiting from anticipated U.S. rate cuts and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop, as well as robust central-bank buying and ETFs inflows. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Betting on steepening government bond yield curves is likely to be rewarding, although many investors favor such trades, Jefferies' Mohit Kumar says in a note. Eurozone bond yield curves steepened Thursday--implying wider spreads between short- and long-dated bond yields--following Germany's upward revision of debt issuance in the fourth quarter. "As we have argued before, we like steepeners, but the whole world likes steepeners," says the global economist. Jefferies had taken a step back from steepeners some days ago tactically--or in the short term--as the trade was getting consensus on Jefferies' positioning indices, Kumar says. "Our medium term view remains one of steepeners, but we would wait for a bit more cleanup in positions and better levels," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0726 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds climb following the release of the public sector finances data. The U.K. public borrowing for August increased to 18 billion pounds, higher than the 13.5 billion-pound consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. "This is going to lead to calls about the sustainability of public finances, and the need for tax rises at the upcoming Budget," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. The 10-year gilt yields climbed 4 basis points earlier to a two-week high of 4.711% before retreating to last trade at 4.700%, Tradeweb data show. The 30-year gilt yields are at 2-week high of 5.549%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0721 GMT - U.K. public-sector borrowing data highlights the deteriorating nature of public finances even if the economy hasn't been terribly weak, Capital Economics' Paul Dales says in a note. Net borrowing came in at 18 billion pounds in August, well above the 12.5 billion pounds expected by the OBR budget watchdog. That is more than 11 billion pounds over the OBR's forecast in March, Dales notes. It suggests the U.K. treasury chief Rachel Reeves will have to raise 28 billion pounds mostly through higher taxes in the budget due in November if she wants to stick to her self-imposed guidelines, he writes. However, there are signs the economy isn't too weak, with separate data showing retail sales rising for a third straight month in August, Dales adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0712 GMT - The long-end driven selloff in bond markets is put to test as a tangible trigger for the 7-8 basis point increase in 30-year eurozone government bond yields on Thursday remains absent, says Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen in a note. The German Finance Agency's funding increase for the fourth quarter "possibly served as an excuse to sell, while upbeat U.S. macro data in the afternoon caused further headwinds from U.S. Treasurys," the rates strategist says. Commerzbank Research sees chances for markets to stabilise, with buyers potentially emerging for German Bunds at yields above 2.70%. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2.8 basis points at 2.742%, while the 30-year Bund yield is up 2.8 basis points at 3.327%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields rise, tracking yields of the U.S. peers. The French 10-year OAT yield rises somewhat less that the 10-year German Bund yield but it could remain prone to further increases as the new French Prime Minister aims to push next year's budget through the parliament. On Thursday, French bonds widened against German peers amid widespread protests in France against the government's spending cut plans. "This increases the pressure on new French PM Lecornu with Lecornu so far having given limited indications of concessions he will make in the 2026 budget to get it approved by parliament," says Danske Research's Jesper Fjarstedt in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2.4 basis points at 2.738%, the 10-year OAT yield is up 1.8 basis points at 3.542%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - Japan's Nikkei Stock Average closed 0.6% lower at 45045.81 after the Bank of Japan said it would start gradually selling its holdings of domestic equity exchange-traded funds and real estate investment trusts. The Tokyo Stock Exchange REIT Index dropped 0.3% to 1931.81. Retail and electronics stocks led the declines. Fast Retailing fell 4.5% and TDK dropped 3.3%. As the BOJ move showed it remains on its tightening path, the two-year Japanese government bond yield rose 2.5 bps to 0.905%, the 10-year yield gained 4.0 bps to 1.635%, while the 30-year yield fell 3.5 bps to 3.155%. USD/JPY was at 147.83, compared with 147.99 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0650 GMT - The dollar edges higher after stronger U.S. data eased fears about a weak economic outlook, particularly in the labor market, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Initial jobless claims fell 33,000 to 231,000 last week, below the 240,000 expected by economists in a WSJ survey. The Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing activity index jumped to 23.2 in September, above the 2.0 forecast. "All round, that helped to lift the mood on the U.S. outlook, particularly after several prints in early September had come in on the downside," the analysts say. The Federal Reserve resumed cutting interest rates Wednesday amid evidence of a cooling labor market. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 97.482. It reached a three-and-a-half-year low of 96.218 Wednesday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - The active rating cycle for eurozone sovereigns is likely to persist with increasing convergence between semicore and peripheral countries, Citi Research's Aman Bansal says in a note. "Year-to-date changes in EMU-11 sovereign ratings have already surpassed annual totals since 2018," the rates strategist says. This gathered pace last week with France's downgrade to A+ stable by Fitch Ratings, alongside upgrades for Portugal (A stable) by Fitch and Spain (A+ stable) by S&P Global Ratings, he says. "For France, this was the first downgrade out of double-A territory and resulted in its lowest average rating since the formation of euro area, while the ratings for Spain and Portugal were taken to their highest since the euro crisis," Bansal says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Malaysia's Exports Face Risks From U.S. Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's trade outlook is cloudy, as U.S. tariffs that took effect in August gradually weigh on exports and manufacturing, RHB senior economist Chin Yee Sian says in a note. Export growth is expected to ease to 3.1% in 2H from 3.8% in 1H, with shipments to the U.S. likely to soften as front-loading fades, she reckons. Full-year export growth could come in at 3.4%, compared with 5.7% in 2024, she says. However, diversified products and markets, easing U.S.-China tensions, and clearer U.S. tariff guidance could provide some cushion, she adds. RHB warns that risks remain from potential sector-specific U.S. levies, particularly semiconductors, which account for about 28% of Malaysia's exports to the U.S. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0803 GMT - Malaysia's trade outlook is cloudy, as U.S. tariffs that took effect in August gradually weigh on exports and manufacturing, RHB senior economist Chin Yee Sian says in a note. On Thursday, French bonds widened against German peers amid widespread protests in France against the government's spending cut plans. "This increases the pressure on new French PM Lecornu with Lecornu so far having given limited indications of concessions he will make in the 2026 budget to get it approved by parliament," says Danske Research's Jesper Fjarstedt in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2.4 basis points at 2.738%, the 10-year OAT yield is up 1.8 basis points at 3.542%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - Japan's Nikkei Stock Average closed 0.6% lower at 45045.81 after the Bank of Japan said it would start gradually selling its holdings of domestic equity exchange-traded funds and real estate investment trusts. The Tokyo Stock Exchange REIT Index dropped 0.3% to 1931.81. Retail and electronics stocks led the declines. Fast Retailing fell 4.5% and TDK dropped 3.3%. As the BOJ move showed it remains on its tightening path, the two-year Japanese government bond yield rose 2.5 bps to 0.905%, the 10-year yield gained 4.0 bps to 1.635%, while the 30-year yield fell 3.5 bps to 3.155%. USD/JPY was at 147.83, compared with 147.99 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0650 GMT - The dollar edges higher after stronger U.S. data eased fears about a weak economic outlook, particularly in the labor market, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Initial jobless claims fell 33,000 to 231,000 last week, below the 240,000 expected by economists in a WSJ survey. The Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing activity index jumped to 23.2 in September, above the 2.0 forecast. "All round, that helped to lift the mood on the U.S. outlook, particularly after several prints in early September had come in on the downside," the analysts say. The Federal Reserve resumed cutting interest rates Wednesday amid evidence of a cooling labor market. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 97.482. It reached a three-and-a-half-year low of 96.218 Wednesday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0800 GMT - Oil prices are on track for a modest weekly gain as a firmer U.S. dollar weighs on sentiment and markets await more clarity on Western sanctions against Russia. In early trade, Brent crude is down 0.2% to $67.28 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.4% to $62.99 a barrel. Both benchmarks are up only 0.5% this week. "Despite modest weekly gains, crude remains stuck in a narrow range since early August, weighed down by bearish fundamentals including OPEC+'s accelerated output return," says Soojin Kim from MUFG. However, concerns persist over potential disruptions to Russian supplies following recent Ukrainian attacks on key energy infrastructure and President Trump's call for NATO allies to halt purchases of Russian oil. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - London-listed miners gain in early morning European trading as gold ticks higher. Gold futures are up 0.2% at $3,685.20 a troy ounce and nearly 10% higher this month on U.S. interest rate cut hopes and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop. Endeavour Mining and Fresnillo are leading the FTSE 100 index risers, up 3% and 2.5%, respectively. Anglo American shares are up 1.3%, while Antofagasta shares are trading 1% higher (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0734 GMT - Angola's crude oil production will likely rise 1.7% in 2026, boosted by new projects that have come on stream ahead of schedule in Africa's second-largest producer, says Oxford Economics Africa. The oil projects, run by France's TotalEnergies and Italy's Eni, helped boost production in August to 1.10 million barrels a day from 1.08 million barrels a day in July, and will continue to offset losses blamed on the country's ageing oil facilities. "We now anticipate that production will reach an estimated 1.11 million barrels a day early next year, bouncing back moderately to a full-year average of 1.14 million barrels a day in 2026," Oxford says. "Overall, the prospect for moderately higher oil output over the medium term is positive." (nicholas.bariyo@wsj.com;@Nicholasbariyo)

0638 GMT - Comex gold futures have likely shifted into a phase of consolidation, based on the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a report. The futures' bearish price action on Thursday confirms strong resistance has formed at $3,700/oz, the analyst says. The commodity's bullish momentum has decelerated, as indicated by the relative strength index, Chai says. Hence, futures could pull back toward the up-trending 20-day simple moving average, the analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.1% higher at $3,646.34/oz.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0612 GMT - Electricity Generating's acquisition of 30% stake in Indonesia's Chandra Daya Investasi unlocks value, CGS International's Rasmiman Sermprasert says in a research report. This stake is expected to contribute at least 11% of the Thai company's total profit per year over 2026-2027, the analyst estimates. Hence, the brokerage lifts its 2026 and 2027 core net profit forecasts for Electricity Generating by 57% and 37%, respectively, reflecting the earnings accretion from the Indonesian company, which is now a publicly listed entity in Indonesia. The brokerage raises the stock's rating to add from hold and the target price to THB130.00 from THB110.00. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB116.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

FORM 8.3

PUBLIC OPENING POSITION DISCLOSURE/DEALING DISCLOSURE BY

Rule 8.3 of the Takeover Code (the "Code")

1.         KEY INFORMATION

(a) Full name of discloser:

(b) Owner or controller of interests and short positions disclosed, if different from 1(a):

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 4 AM ET: ADB, IFC in Talks to Back Maynilad Water IPO as Cornerstone Investors | Under ...
data.description:
ADB, IFC in Talks to Back Maynilad Water IPO as Cornerstone Investors

Two multilateral funding agencies are set to take a significant stake in Philippine utility Maynilad Water Services ahead of its initial public offering, sources said.

----

Under Stress, World's Top Exporter of Steelmaking Coal Cuts Jobs, Shuts Pits

Rising operating costs, falling coal prices and high royalties are straining Australia's massive steelmaking coal mines, triggering layoffs and pit closures.

----

Exxon Sparks Investor Rights Battle. Why It Could Spread.

The oil giant has unveiled a system to let investors automatically support management proposals. Activists are alarmed.

----

Oil Futures Post Back-to-Back Losses

Oil futures added to yesterday's losses as the high-demand summer season began to wind down and the market waited to see what if any additional sanctions were placed on Russian oil.

----

U.S. Natural Gas Falls on Above-Estimate Storage Build

U.S. natural gas futures fell after the EIA reported an above-consensus 90 billion-cubic-foot increase in underground storage, extending the inventory surplus over the five-year average to more than 200 Bcf.

----

California Wants to Halt Oil Industry Exodus After Years of Climate Focus

Policymakers are trying to stave off a potential fuel-supply crunch while refineries look to close.

----

Exxon Mobil to Halt European Recycling Plant Investments

The company is halting more than $100 million in planned investments for two European chemical recycling projects because of concerns over new rules that the company says will undermine the projects' business case.

----

Santos Confirms XRG Consortium Pulls $18.7 Billion Takeover Bid

Santos confirmed that a consortium led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. has abandoned a $18.7 billion takeover offer, highlighting disagreements over terms given the length of time it could take for a deal to complete.

----

Mexican Government Issues Debt to Finance Pemex Buybacks

The Mexican government said it issued $13.8 billion in debt in U.S. dollars and euros as part of a series of transactions aimed at capitalizing and shoring up the finances of state oil company Petróleos Mexicanos.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Santos and oil futures in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Post Large Decline

Crude oil inventories fell by 9.3 million barrels to 415.4 million barrels in the week ended Sept. 12 as exports almost doubled and imports fell.

----

Beleaguered Offshore Wind Gets Two Small Signs of Hope

The Department of Justice filed a legal brief earlier this month supporting Empire Wind, an offshore wind project near New York's Long Island that the administration had previously halted.

----

Oil, Gas Investment Needed to Keep Output Steady as Field Declines Accelerate, IEA Says

The world must invest an average of$540 billion through 2050 in the upstream sector or face a reduced global production of 5.5 million barrels a day for the next decade, a new report stated.

----

Energy Department Awards BWX $1.5 Billion Contract

A BWX Technologies unit was awarded a National Nuclear Security Administration contract for work on a Domestic Uranium Enrichment Centrifuge Experiment pilot plant.

data.title: Centrica's Energy Trading Business Expands U.S. Presence With New York Office
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Centrica's energy trading and optimization unit opened a gas trading office in New York as part of its plans to expand its footprint across North America.

The office will serve as a hub for Centrica Energy's physical gas trading activities in the U.S. and will support Centrica's plan to establish an integrated gas, power, and certificates risk management business in the U.S.

"The U.S. energy market is undergoing rapid change, creating demand for more agile and efficient trading solutions," the company said Friday. "Centrica Energy sees strong potential to apply its global expertise to help balance increasingly volatile power markets and optimize gas flows."

Industry veteran Chris Silva has been appointed as head of U.S. physical gas trading while Heather Mason will serve as U.S. chief operating officer, integrating Centrica Energy's global trading capabilities into the U.S. market, it said.

Centrica Energy is responsible for managing commodity risk and sourcing energy on behalf of Centrica Group's energy supply activities. It is an energy trading company which helps move energy from source to use, providing power to homes and businesses.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com
